result:
I.  
The first light today came not from the sun but from the screen beside my bed, a soft rectangle of blue that said 5:17 a.m. and offered, like a butler, a list of things I might care about before breakfast: a missile test in the Sea of Japan, a sale on noise-canceling headphones, a notification that someone I barely knew had “added to their story.” I touched the glass and the world shrank to the size of my palm, a tidy cosmos in which everything urgent is also trivial. While I was scrolling, the real sky went from charcoal to pewter to rose without my assistance. By the time I looked up, the performance was over, the clouds already folding themselves into ordinary daylight. I felt the slight embarrassment of having refused a gift that was offered freely, quietly, with no tracking cookie or unsubscribe link.

II.  
Years ago I owned a clock that ticked. It was loud enough to intrude on insomnia, each second landing like a dropped marble in an empty room. One night I moved the clock to the kitchen and closed the bedroom door, but the heartbeat followed me; the apartment was too small for silence. So I took the batteries out and laid them neatly beside the clock, like organs beside a corpse. The next morning I woke in a panic anyway, convinced I had overslept, and I realized the ticking had never been the problem—time was. Without the audible tally I was still counting, still bargaining: five more minutes, one more day, another year before the novel is finished, the mortgage burned, the parents buried. The batteries stayed out; the clock became a paperweight, a monument to the moment I tried to arrest motion by unplugging it.

III.  
I once read that the human brain edits memory each time it retrieves it, the way a librarian might re-shelve a stolen book with marginalia added by the thief. This explains why childhood summers elongate each time I revisit them, the backyard hydrangeas growing bluer, the mosquito bites multiplying into constellations. My mother’s voice, too, has shifted key, from alto to something closer to soprano, as though memory decided grief should harmonize upward. I can no longer separate what happened from what I have told myself happened, and perhaps that is why I keep telling: the story is a raft I keep adding boards to, hoping it will still float if the river widens.

IV.  
There is a kind of loneliness that arrives at 2:14 p.m. on a Tuesday when every inbox is empty, the neighbors’ laundry is done, and the cat has folded itself into a loaf and refuses to audition for companionship. It is not the midnight variety that howls; it is a daylight loneliness that hums like fluorescent tubing, so steady you almost stop hearing it. In that hour I have been known to open the refrigerator as if the answer might be behind the pickles, or to speak aloud the name of a friend who lives twelve time zones away, testing whether the syllables still taste like friendship when they echo back unwitnessed. The strange comfort is discovering I am boring even to myself; the spell breaks, I boil water for tea, I return to the page.

V.  
I write on a machine that remembers everything except intent. It saves each version, each false start, so that buried in folders are manuscripts called “Novel_final” followed by “Novel_final_FINAL_use_this_one.” Sometimes I open the earlier files to see who I was on March 9, 2016, at 11:42 p.m.—a person who thought the solution was to move chapter four before chapter two, who believed the problem was sequence rather than substance. That earlier me is as dead as the Neanderthal who once placed a hand on a cave wall and blew pigment around it, leaving a negative space that says I was here. The computer keeps my ghosts in alphabetical order; I scroll past them like a tourist studying tombstones written in his own handwriting.

VI.  
When my father was dying, he asked for a notebook and wrote numbers in it, page after page of digits that looked like code but were probably just the collapsing architecture of a mind trying to inventory what it was losing. I kept the notebook for a long time, thinking someday I would decipher it, discover a final message intended only for me. Eventually I lost the notebook during a move, and its disappearance felt like mercy: the burden of meaning evaporated, replaced by the simpler grief of absence. Now I wonder if the numbers were not inventory but music, a score he heard as the lights dimmed, something to hum while waiting for the curtain.

VII.  
I have never learned to drive a stick shift, knot a bowtie, or finish a crossword without cheating. These lacunae feel personal, as though the universe held three private grudges. In dreams I sometimes operate a vehicle whose gears grind like angry teeth, and I wake relieved that the emergency brake is just the cat sleeping on my feet. The bowtie I will never need unless invited to a costume ball where everyone pretends to be twentieth-century novelists; I would go as Virginia Woolf and spend the evening explaining that the river was a metaphor, not a destination. The crossword I attempt daily, a vaccine against dementia administered in seven-letter doses. I tell myself that knowledge is cumulative, that yesterday’s ignorance of the capital of Eritrea is today’s triumph when I finally retain Asmara, but the next day the clue is back, blank squares yawning like missing teeth, and I forgive myself for forgetting what I was never required to know.

VIII.  
At the farmer’s market I become a better version of myself: someone who asks how the kale was grown, who remembers to bring canvas bags, who does not flinch at the price of honey. I chat with the baker about sourdough starters as though I, too, nurture a bubbling jar on my counter, rather than store-bought slices in a plastic sack. The exchange is theater, but the loaves are warm, and for the length of a city block I believe in the possibility of transformation through good intentions and whole grains. By the time I reach the car the kale has wilted, the baguette’s heel is missing, and I am once again the person who lets bananas blacken before remembering they exist. Still, every Saturday I return to the stalls like a parishioner, seeking absolution in the form of a perfect peach.

IX.  
Language keeps making promises it can’t keep. We say “the speed of light” as though light were late for an appointment, “butterfly effect” as though insects filed flight plans. I once told someone I would love them “forever,” and we both pretended forever was a measurement rather than a metaphor, something you could pour into a calendar and seal with a kiss. Later, when the love had become a photograph creased along the fold of our bodies, I tried to calculate the half-life of promises. The math eluded me, but the sentence lingered, radioactive, glowing softly in the dark of new solitude. Now I hesitate before declaring even the weather, afraid the clouds will take me literally and rain out of spite.

X.  
Here is what I know how to do: boil an egg so the yolk stays custard, whistle through my teeth, recite the prologue to The Canterbury Tales in Middle English which is useless except it proves I was once young enough to memorize without understanding. Here is what I do not know: how to let go without looking back, how to hear the neighbor’s baby cry at 3 a.m. without constructing narratives about futures that will never include me, how to finish a letter with anything warmer or colder than “sincerely.” The list of unknowns is longer, but the balance shifts if I measure in wonder instead of certainty. The egg, after all, is a small sun cracked open; the whistle travels farther than my voice; Chaucer’s vowels still echo in a tongue I never spoke except to pledge I could retain what beauty insisted on keeping, even after the exam was over.

XI.  
The day will come when someone else empties my drawers, puzzling over why I saved a single dice, a ticket stub, a river of receipts. They will not know that the dice was rolled to decide whether to call the doctor, that the stub was from a play where an actor looked directly at me during a monologue about forgiveness, that the receipts were proof I once ate alone in daylight and paid without shame. All artifacts will migrate to trash or thrift, and that, too, is a form of publication: the essay of a life released from authorship, left to be footnoted by strangers. I picture a graduate student one hundred winters from now, cursor blinking over a dissertation titled “Material Ephemera of the Early Digital Age,” and I want to tell her that the cracked coffee mug was never symbolic, only beloved, but symbolism is what arrives when the hand that held the handle is gone.

XII.  
Meanwhile, the afternoon light slides across the desk like a slow tide, and the page (digital or paper, it hardly matters) waits for the next syllable. I write because it is the only form of time travel I have perfected, a way to haunt rooms I no longer occupy, to hand the dice to you, reader, who may roll it or pocket it or discard it entirely. The choice is yours; the sentence ends here, the clock without batteries continues not to tick, and somewhere a hydrangea is turning an impossible shade that only the sky remembers seeing.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
